B) Place each SSID on a dedicated VLAN with inter-VLAN routing enforced through a firewall that applies least-privilege communication rules between segments — isolating IoT devices from corporate and guest traffic; implement WPA3-Enterprise with 802.1X for the corporate SSID to provide per-user authentication; implement WPA3-Personal with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) for the guest SSID with regular passphrase rotation or transition to an onboarding portal that issues per-session credentials; and isolate the IoT VLAN from all other segments with an explicit deny-all default policy, permitting only the specific protocols required for IoT device management
C) Implement MAC address filtering on all three SSIDs to restrict network access to pre-approved device hardware addresses, and deploy a wireless proxy that inspects all traffic crossing SSID boundaries before permitting inter-network communication
D) Migrate all three SSIDs to a single unified SSID with dynamic VLAN assignment based on 802.1X authentication results, so that managed devices are assigned to the corporate VLAN, guest devices to the guest VLAN, and IoT devices to the IoT VLAN through a single authentication infrastructure
Correct: B
Explanation: The three findings require three distinct architectural remediation streams. Finding 1 — shared Layer 2 broadcast domain: placing all SSIDs on the same Layer 2 domain means any device on any SSID can communicate directly with devices on other SSIDs without routing — a guest visitor can reach IoT devices, and a compromised IoT device can reach corporate endpoints. VLAN segmentation creates Layer 2 isolation between the three networks, and firewall enforcement at the inter-VLAN routing boundary applies least-privilege policies — the IoT VLAN in particular should have a default-deny policy with only the specific management protocols required explicitly permitted. Finding 2 — IoT cleartext credential transmission: isolating the IoT VLAN from all other segments (including corporate and guest) contains the exposure of the cleartext protocol to the IoT segment only — no other network segment can observe the IoT traffic. This is a compensating control for a protocol that cannot be changed (legacy IoT devices frequently cannot be upgraded). The explicit deny-all with specific protocol allowlist further limits the blast radius if an IoT device is compromised. Finding 3 — WPA2-Personal with stale passphrase: WPA2-Personal's core weakness is that all devices share the same key, meaning a single compromised device or disclosed passphrase gives network access to all users. WPA3-Personal with SAE significantly improves this by providing forward secrecy (each session uses a unique key derived through SAE, so capturing traffic before obtaining the passphrase does not decrypt it retroactively) and resistance to offline dictionary attacks. Regular rotation or per-session credentials through an onboarding portal further reduces the stale passphrase risk.
Why A is wrong: Upgrading all three SSIDs to WPA3-Enterprise with certificate-based authentication addresses authentication strength but does not resolve the Layer 2 broadcast domain problem — all three SSIDs would still be on the same network segment, allowing cross-SSID communication regardless of authentication method. IoT devices typically cannot support 802.1X certificate-based authentication, making WPA3-Enterprise impractical for the IoT SSID without device replacement. WIDS for rogue AP detection is a valuable monitoring control but does not address the segmentation, protocol, or passphrase findings. This option addresses authentication without addressing the network architecture problem.
Why C is wrong: MAC address filtering is a weak security control that provides minimal protection — MAC addresses are transmitted in cleartext in wireless frames and can be trivially spoofed by any attacker using a wireless adapter in monitor mode. An attacker who observes a valid MAC address on the network can clone it and bypass MAC filtering entirely. MAC filtering also creates significant administrative overhead for large or dynamic device populations. A wireless proxy inspecting cross-SSID traffic is not a standard architectural component and does not address the Layer 2 broadcast domain issue — inter-SSID traffic visibility and control requires VLAN segmentation and firewall enforcement at the routing boundary, not a proxy.
Why D is wrong: Unifying all three SSIDs into a single SSID with 802.1X-based dynamic VLAN assignment is a viable architecture for corporate and guest devices, but it fundamentally fails for IoT devices, which typically cannot perform 802.1X authentication. IoT devices would not be assignable to the IoT VLAN through this mechanism and would either fail to connect or fall into a default VLAN that may not provide appropriate isolation. Additionally, a single SSID with dynamic VLAN assignment still requires the underlying VLAN segmentation and inter-VLAN firewall enforcement described in option B — the single-SSID approach is a user experience simplification that sits on top of the same segmentation architecture, not an alternative to it.

Domain: 3.0 Security Architecture
Sub-objective: 3.3 — Compare and contrast concepts and strategies to protect data
Question: An organization processes highly sensitive merger and acquisition documents. Legal counsel and the security team require that: documents cannot be printed or screen-captured; access to documents must expire automatically 30 days after sharing, even if the recipient has already downloaded the file; and all access events must be logged with user identity and timestamp regardless of where the file is opened. A standard file encryption solution that requires a decryption key to open the file is proposed by an IT administrator. The security team rejects this proposal. Which technology precisely meets all three requirements, and why is standard file encryption insufficient?
A) Standard file encryption with a strong key management system meets all three requirements — key expiration policies can enforce the 30-day access window, the encryption algorithm prevents printing and screen capture, and key access logs provide the required audit trail; the security team's rejection reflects a misunderstanding of modern encryption capabilities
B) Information Rights Management (IRM) / Enterprise Digital Rights Management (EDRM) — a technology that embeds persistent usage policy enforcement within the document itself, enforced by a rights management client or service at the time of access regardless of file location; IRM can enforce print and screen capture restrictions through DRM controls in the viewing application, enforce automatic access expiration by requiring online license validation against a rights management server at each access attempt, and log every access event with identity and timestamp to a centralized audit service; standard file encryption is insufficient because it is a binary control — once the decryption key is provided, the recipient has unrestricted access to the plaintext with no enforcement of usage restrictions, no automatic expiration, and no access logging
C) A secure file sharing portal that requires recipients to access documents through a web browser interface only, with the portal enforcing session-based access controls, browser-level print restrictions, and server-side access logging; standard file encryption is insufficient because encrypted files cannot be indexed by search engines, reducing document discoverability
D) Blockchain-based document notarization that records document access events as immutable transactions, with smart contracts enforcing the 30-day expiration by automatically revoking the document hash after 30 days; standard file encryption is insufficient because it does not provide tamper-evident audit logs that satisfy legal evidentiary standards
Correct: B
Explanation: The three requirements share a common characteristic: they must be enforced persistently after the file has left the organization's control — after download to a recipient's device. This is the critical architectural requirement that eliminates most standard security controls. Information Rights Management (IRM), also called Enterprise Digital Rights Management (EDRM), embeds policy enforcement within the document or enforces it through a rights management client that must contact a licensing server to open the file. Print restriction: IRM-enabled viewing applications (Microsoft Office with Azure Information Protection, Adobe Acrobat with Rights Management) enforce print and screen capture restrictions at the application layer — the document cannot be printed through the normal print path and screen capture APIs return blank or watermarked output. 30-day access expiration: because the IRM client must contact the rights management server to obtain a use license each time the document is opened, the server can refuse license issuance after the expiration date — the file becomes inaccessible even if already downloaded, because access requires a current valid license from the server. Access logging: every license request to the rights management server generates an authenticated log entry with user identity (tied to the IRM authentication), timestamp, document identifier, and access outcome. Standard encryption is insufficient precisely because it does not persist policy after decryption — once a recipient decrypts the file with the provided key, they have unrestricted plaintext with no mechanism to enforce print restrictions, no server callback to enforce expiration, and no access logging.
Why A is wrong: Standard encryption with key management does not meet any of the three requirements as stated. Key expiration can prevent new decryption operations after expiration, but it does not expire access to a file that was already decrypted and saved as plaintext — a recipient who decrypted the file on day 1 retains full access to the plaintext copy indefinitely. Encryption algorithms do not prevent printing or screen capture of decrypted content — once the file is open in an application, all standard OS functions (print, screenshot) operate normally on the plaintext. Key access logs record when the key was retrieved but do not log each time the already-decrypted document is subsequently opened or viewed. The security team's rejection is fully justified and reflects a correct understanding of encryption's limitations for persistent usage policy enforcement.
Why C is wrong: A secure web portal enforces access controls effectively while the recipient accesses documents through the portal interface. However, the scenario specifies that requirements must hold even after the recipient has already downloaded the file — portal-based controls cease to apply once the file is saved to the recipient's local device. Browser-level print restrictions are easily circumvented (browser developer tools, OS-level print to PDF, screen capture) and do not meet the requirement for robust print prevention. Portal access logging covers portal sessions but not offline access to downloaded copies. The portal approach addresses online access but fails the post-download enforcement requirement central to the scenario.
Why D is wrong: Blockchain-based document notarization records document hashes and access events as immutable ledger entries — this is valuable for tamper-evident audit trails and document integrity verification. However, blockchain does not enforce usage restrictions on document content. A smart contract revoking a document hash does not prevent a recipient from opening a previously downloaded file — the blockchain revocation has no enforcement mechanism on the recipient's local device. Blockchain provides an immutable record of events that occurred but cannot enforce what actions are taken on a local file. The print restriction, local access expiration, and real-time access logging requirements cannot be satisfied by blockchain notarization.

Domain: 3.0 Security Architecture
Sub-objective: 3.4 — Explain the importance of resilience and recovery in security architecture
Question: An organization's security architect is designing a resilient architecture for a critical payment processing system that must maintain availability during both infrastructure failures and active security incidents. The architect identifies four availability requirements: the system must survive the failure of any single data center; during a ransomware attack, clean backups must be available that cannot be encrypted by malware with access to production systems; backup integrity must be verified regularly rather than discovered to be corrupt only during a recovery event; and the organization must be able to demonstrate the actual recovery time to auditors rather than a theoretical estimate. Which combination of architectural and operational controls addresses all four requirements?
A) Deploy the payment system across two data centers in an active-passive configuration with synchronous replication; store backups on a network share accessible from the production environment for fast recovery; perform monthly backup restoration tests in the production environment; and document the theoretical RTO based on the replication lag and estimated restoration time
B) Deploy the payment system across two or more data centers in an active-active or active-passive configuration with automated failover to meet the single-datacenter failure requirement; implement immutable backup storage using object storage with object lock (WORM — Write Once Read Many) or an air-gapped offline backup copy that production systems cannot write to or delete from, preventing ransomware from encrypting or deleting backups; implement automated backup integrity verification through scheduled restoration tests in an isolated recovery environment with documented pass/fail results; and conduct periodic full recovery exercises that measure actual recovery time from declared incident to restored operation, producing documented evidence of real RTO for auditors
C) Deploy the payment system in a single data center with redundant hardware components (dual power supplies, RAID storage, redundant network links) to eliminate single points of failure within the data center; encrypt all backup data with a key stored in the production key management system; perform annual disaster recovery tabletop exercises that walk through the recovery procedure without actually restoring systems; and submit the tabletop exercise results to auditors as evidence of recovery capability
D) Deploy the payment system across two data centers with asynchronous replication to minimize performance impact; store backups in a cloud storage bucket with versioning enabled so that previous versions can be retrieved if current files are encrypted; test backup restoration annually by restoring a single non-critical file to verify the backup system is functional; and provide auditors with the vendor's published SLA uptime guarantee as evidence of recovery capability
Correct: B
Explanation: Each of the four requirements maps to a specific architectural or operational control. Requirement 1 — survive single data center failure: active-active or active-passive multi-datacenter deployment with automated failover ensures that loss of one data center does not interrupt service. Active-active provides the highest availability (both sites serve traffic simultaneously); active-passive provides failover with some recovery time. Both satisfy the single-datacenter failure requirement. Requirement 2 — ransomware-resistant backups: immutable backup storage (WORM object lock in cloud storage such as AWS S3 Object Lock or Azure Immutable Blob Storage) prevents any process — including ransomware with compromised production credentials — from modifying or deleting backup objects during the retention period. An air-gapped offline backup copy provides equivalent protection through physical separation. The critical architectural principle is that production systems must not have write or delete access to backup storage — the backup medium must be unreachable from the production environment through any credential or network path available to ransomware. Requirement 3 — verified backup integrity: scheduled automated restoration tests in an isolated environment detect corruption, encryption failures, or incomplete backups before they are needed in a real recovery scenario. Requirement 4 — demonstrated actual RTO: only a full recovery exercise that measures clock time from declaration to restored operation produces evidence of actual RTO. Theoretical estimates and tabletop exercises produce documented procedures but not empirical time measurements.
Why A is wrong: Storing backups on a network share accessible from the production environment directly violates the ransomware-resistant backup requirement — ransomware that compromises production credentials will encrypt or delete network-accessible backup shares. This is one of the most common ransomware attack patterns and a well-documented backup destruction technique. Monthly restoration tests in the production environment contaminate the production system with test data and do not provide an isolated, auditable integrity verification result. Documenting theoretical RTO explicitly fails the fourth requirement, which calls for demonstrated actual recovery time.
Why C is wrong: Single data center deployment with redundant hardware addresses component-level failures within a data center (power supply failure, disk failure) but does not survive a complete data center outage — fire, flood, power grid failure, or a catastrophic infrastructure event affecting the entire facility would take the system offline. This fails the first requirement. Encrypting backups with a key stored in the production key management system means ransomware that compromises the production KMS can render backups inaccessible — backup encryption keys must be protected independently from the production environment. Annual tabletop exercises that do not restore systems produce no evidence of actual recovery time and fail the fourth requirement.
Why D is wrong: Asynchronous replication introduces replication lag — data committed to the primary site may not yet have been replicated to the secondary when a failure occurs, meaning some transactions may be lost. While asynchronous replication satisfies availability requirements in many contexts, the payment processing scenario implies data integrity requirements where transaction loss is unacceptable. Cloud storage versioning with current-account access does not provide ransomware-resistant backups — ransomware operating with compromised cloud credentials can delete all versions if the storage account permits version deletion, which standard versioning does not prevent. Restoring a single non-critical file annually does not verify full system recovery capability. Vendor SLA uptime guarantees are contractual commitments, not evidence of the organization's own recovery capability — auditors require evidence that the organization can actually recover its systems, not that the infrastructure provider promises availability.

Domain: 1.0 General Security Concepts
Sub-objective: 1.1 — Compare and contrast various types of security controls
Question: A healthcare organization is implementing a layered security program for its electronic health record (EHR) system. The security committee reviews six proposed controls and must classify each correctly before approving the budget. The controls are: (1) a policy requiring all workforce members to complete HIPAA privacy training before being granted EHR access; (2) a data backup system that creates daily encrypted copies of all EHR data to an offsite facility; (3) a screen lock that automatically activates after 90 seconds of workstation inactivity; (4) security cameras monitoring all server room entry points with footage retained for 90 days; (5) biometric fingerprint scanners on server room doors that prevent entry without an enrolled fingerprint; (6) a process for notifying affected patients and HHS within required timeframes following a confirmed breach. Which classification correctly identifies the control category for all six controls?
A) (1) Administrative preventive; (2) Technical corrective; (3) Technical preventive; (4) Physical detective; (5) Physical preventive; (6) Administrative corrective
B) (1) Administrative preventive; (2) Technical corrective; (3) Technical detective; (4) Physical preventive; (5) Physical detective; (6) Administrative compensating
C) (1) Administrative detective; (2) Technical preventive; (3) Physical preventive; (4) Technical detective; (5) Administrative preventive; (6) Physical corrective
D) (1) Administrative deterrent; (2) Administrative corrective; (3) Technical preventive; (4) Physical detective; (5) Physical preventive; (6) Administrative corrective
Correct: A
Explanation: Security controls are classified by two intersecting dimensions: implementation type (administrative, technical, physical) and functional category (preventive, detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating, directive). Applying both dimensions to each control: Control 1 — HIPAA training policy: Administrative (implemented through policy and procedure, not technology or physical means) and Preventive (it is designed to prevent unauthorized or improper access and handling by ensuring workforce members understand their obligations before receiving access — a precondition that prevents violations). Control 2 — offsite encrypted backup: Technical (implemented through technology systems) and Corrective (backups do not prevent incidents or detect them — their function is to restore systems and data after an incident has occurred, correcting the damage). Control 3 — automatic screen lock: Technical (implemented through OS or application configuration) and Preventive (it prevents unauthorized users from accessing an unattended authenticated session — it stops a potential access violation before it occurs). Control 4 — security cameras: Physical (cameras are physical monitoring devices) and Detective (cameras do not prevent entry or access — they record and provide evidence of events that have occurred, enabling detection and investigation). Control 5 — biometric door scanners: Physical (physical access control hardware) and Preventive (biometric scanners actively deny entry to unenrolled individuals — they prevent unauthorized physical access from occurring). Control 6 — breach notification process: Administrative (a defined procedural process) and Corrective (notification does not prevent the breach or detect it — it is a response action taken after a confirmed breach to address the harm, notify affected parties, and fulfill regulatory obligations — correcting the situation following an incident).
Why B is wrong: Control 3 (screen lock) is misclassified as detective. A screen lock does not detect anything — it does not monitor, log, or alert. It actively prevents an unauthorized user from accessing an unattended session by requiring re-authentication. Prevention is the correct functional classification. Control 4 (cameras) is misclassified as preventive — cameras have a deterrent effect but their primary functional classification is detective because their core capability is recording and providing evidence, not physically blocking access. Control 5 (biometric scanner) is misclassified as detective — biometric scanners actively deny entry to unauthorized individuals, which is the definition of a preventive control. Control 6 is misclassified as compensating — compensating controls are alternative controls implemented when a primary control cannot be applied; breach notification is a response and remediation process following an incident, which is corrective.
Why C is wrong: Control 1 (training policy) is misclassified as detective — training before access is granted is a precondition designed to prevent violations, not to detect them. Control 2 (backup) is misclassified as preventive — backups do not prevent incidents; they enable recovery after incidents, which is corrective. Control 3 (screen lock) is misclassified as physical — a software-enforced screen lock is a technical control, not a physical one. Control 4 (cameras) is misclassified as technical — cameras are physical devices in the physical environment. Control 5 (biometric scanner) is misclassified as administrative — a physical hardware scanner is a physical control, not an administrative one. Control 6 is misclassified as physical — a notification process is administrative.
Why D is wrong: Control 1 (training policy) is misclassified as deterrent — a deterrent control discourages potential attackers through the threat of consequence (warning banners, visible cameras, prosecution notices). A training prerequisite for access is a preventive administrative control that proactively builds compliance capability, not a deterrent. Control 2 (backup) is misclassified as administrative — backups are implemented through technology systems, making them technical controls. The corrective functional classification is correct, but the implementation type is wrong.

Domain: 1.0 General Security Concepts
Sub-objective: 1.2 — Summarize fundamental security concepts
Question: A developer is building an API that will be consumed by third-party applications. The API must ensure that requests claiming to come from authorized applications are genuinely from those applications, that transmitted data cannot be read by intermediaries, that a received request cannot be resubmitted by an attacker who captured it to trigger the same action again, and that the application sending a request cannot later deny having sent it. Which combination of cryptographic and protocol mechanisms most precisely addresses all four requirements?
A) TLS mutual authentication (mTLS) for transport encryption and client certificate verification; HMAC-SHA256 request signing with a timestamp and nonce included in each request; and asymmetric signing of request payloads with the client's private key, producing a verifiable signature that provides non-repudiation
B) API key authentication transmitted in the Authorization header over HTTPS; server-side rate limiting to prevent replay of captured requests; and server-side request logging to provide an audit trail that supports non-repudiation claims
C) OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens transmitted over TLS; token expiration set to 60 seconds to limit the replay window; and token issuance logs maintained by the authorization server to support non-repudiation
D) Symmetric AES-256 encryption of all request payloads with a shared secret; a sequence number included in each request that the server validates to detect replayed requests; and digital certificates issued to each client application to provide non-repudiation
Correct: A
Explanation: Each requirement maps to a specific cryptographic or protocol mechanism. Requirement 1 — verify requests are genuinely from authorized applications (authentication): mTLS requires both the server and client to present certificates during the TLS handshake — the server authenticates the client by validating its certificate against a trusted CA, confirming the request originates from an authorized application holding the corresponding private key. This is stronger than API keys (which can be stolen and replayed) because the private key never leaves the client. Requirement 2 — data cannot be read by intermediaries (confidentiality): TLS encrypts the transport layer, ensuring that intercepted traffic is unreadable. mTLS provides this as part of its operation. Requirement 3 — captured requests cannot be replayed (replay prevention): HMAC request signing with a timestamp and nonce (a random value used once) addresses replay attacks. The server validates that the timestamp is within an acceptable window and that the nonce has not been seen before — a captured and resubmitted request will either have an expired timestamp or a previously used nonce, both of which the server rejects. Requirement 4 — sender cannot deny having sent a request (non-repudiation): asymmetric signing with the client's private key produces a signature that only the holder of that private key could have generated. Because private keys are not shared, the client cannot credibly deny having signed a request — the signature is cryptographic proof of origin. Together these mechanisms address all four requirements with precisely appropriate cryptographic tools.
Why B is wrong: API keys transmitted over HTTPS address confidentiality in transit and provide basic authentication, but API keys are shared secrets — if the API key is compromised, any holder can impersonate the authorized application, and the original application can claim the key was stolen. API keys do not provide non-repudiation because they are not bound to a specific entity through asymmetric cryptography. Server-side rate limiting reduces the window for replay attacks but does not prevent replay of a single captured request — a replayed request within the rate limit will succeed. Server-side logging supports forensic investigation but does not cryptographically prevent denial — a client can still claim the logged request was not sent by them without a cryptographic signature binding the request to their identity.
Why C is wrong: OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens over TLS provide confidentiality and authorization but bearer tokens are not inherently bound to the client's identity — any party in possession of a valid bearer token can use it, which is why they are called "bearer" tokens. This means a stolen token can be used by an attacker without the server detecting the difference, and the legitimate token holder can deny the use. Short token expiration (60 seconds) significantly reduces the replay window but does not eliminate replay — a captured token can be replayed within its validity window. Token issuance logs show that a token was issued to an identity but do not cryptographically prevent the token holder from denying a specific request made with that token, because the token itself carries no signature of the specific request content.
Why D is wrong: Symmetric AES-256 encryption of payloads provides confidentiality but requires both parties to share the encryption key — the fundamental limitation of symmetric cryptography for non-repudiation is that if both parties share the key, either party could have generated any given encrypted message, so the sender can claim the receiver fabricated the message. Non-repudiation requires asymmetric cryptography where only the sender's private key could have produced the signature. Sequence numbers for replay prevention are functional but more fragile than nonce-based approaches — sequence numbers require the server to maintain state for each client and can be disrupted by legitimate out-of-order requests. Digital certificates alone do not provide non-repudiation unless they are used to sign specific request payloads — possession of a certificate proves identity in the TLS handshake but does not produce per-request signatures that bind the certificate holder to specific API actions.

Domain: 1.0 General Security Concepts
Sub-objective: 1.3 — Explain the importance of using appropriate cryptographic solutions
Question: A security engineer is evaluating the organization's public key infrastructure for several use cases. For each use case, the engineer must select the appropriate key type, length, and algorithm. The use cases are: (A) securing TLS connections to a high-traffic public web server requiring strong security with optimal performance; (B) signing software releases to provide integrity and authenticity assurance to customers who download the software; (C) encrypting a symmetric key for secure transmission to a recipient who will use it to decrypt a large dataset. Which combination of algorithm selections is most appropriate for all three use cases, and what is the key property that makes elliptic curve algorithms particularly suitable for use case A?
A) Use case A — RSA-2048 for TLS; Use case B — RSA-4096 for code signing; Use case C — RSA-2048 for key encipherment; elliptic curve algorithms are not preferred for high-traffic servers because their mathematical complexity makes them slower than RSA at equivalent key lengths
B) Use case A — ECDSA with P-256 or P-384 for TLS authentication combined with ECDHE for key exchange; Use case B — RSA-4096 or ECDSA with P-384 for code signing; Use case C — RSA-2048 or higher, or ECIES, for asymmetric encryption of the symmetric key; elliptic curve algorithms are particularly suited for high-traffic TLS because they provide equivalent or stronger security at significantly shorter key lengths, resulting in faster key generation, smaller certificate sizes, reduced handshake computation, and lower CPU overhead at scale — critical performance characteristics for servers handling large numbers of concurrent TLS connections
C) Use case A — AES-256 for TLS session encryption; Use case B — SHA-256 for code signing; Use case C — AES-256 for encrypting the symmetric key; elliptic curve algorithms are suitable for Use case A because AES is a symmetric algorithm that shares key material efficiently between endpoints
D) Use case A — RSA-1024 for TLS to maximize handshake performance through minimal key size; Use case B — MD5 with RSA for code signing because MD5 produces a compact digest that minimizes signature size; Use case C — Diffie-Hellman key exchange to encrypt the symmetric key for transmission; elliptic curve algorithms are preferred for high-traffic servers because 1024-bit RSA and elliptic curve keys are equivalent in security strength
Correct: B
Explanation: Each use case has specific algorithm requirements driven by the security and performance characteristics needed. Use case A — high-traffic TLS: TLS authentication uses asymmetric algorithms for the certificate and key exchange. ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm) with NIST curves P-256 or P-384 is the modern standard for TLS certificates on high-traffic servers. ECDHE (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral) provides forward secrecy for key exchange — ephemeral keys mean each session uses a unique key pair, so compromise of the server's long-term private key does not decrypt previously recorded sessions. The key property making ECC suitable for high-traffic use: ECC provides equivalent security to RSA at dramatically shorter key lengths — a 256-bit ECC key provides approximately equivalent security to a 3072-bit RSA key. Shorter keys mean faster mathematical operations, smaller TLS handshake messages, and significantly lower CPU consumption per handshake — critical at scale where a server handles thousands of simultaneous TLS negotiations. Use case B — code signing: RSA-4096 or ECDSA P-384 are appropriate for long-lived code signing use cases where signatures must remain trustworthy for years — higher key lengths provide security margin against future cryptanalytic advances. Use case C — symmetric key encipherment: RSA or ECIES (Elliptic Curve Integrated Encryption Scheme) are appropriate for encrypting a symmetric key for transmission — the small size of a symmetric key (128, 256 bits) makes asymmetric encryption of the key itself computationally feasible, enabling secure key exchange without a shared secret established in advance.
Why A is wrong: RSA-2048 for TLS is a functional but increasingly dated choice — current NIST guidance recommends transitioning from RSA-2048 to either RSA-3072+ or ECC P-256+ for new deployments. The claim that elliptic curve algorithms are slower than RSA at equivalent key lengths is factually reversed — ECC is significantly faster than RSA at equivalent security levels because ECC achieves equivalent security with much shorter keys, reducing the computational cost of modular arithmetic operations. This is precisely why ECC is preferred for high-traffic TLS servers. RSA-2048 for key encipherment in Use case C is functional but does not reflect the performance advantage of ECC-based key encipherment for equivalent security.
Why C is wrong: AES-256 is a symmetric encryption algorithm — it cannot be used for TLS certificate authentication or asymmetric key exchange. TLS uses asymmetric algorithms (RSA, ECDSA) for authentication and key exchange, with symmetric algorithms (AES) for the bulk data encryption that follows the handshake. SHA-256 is a hash function used to produce a message digest — it is not a signing algorithm by itself and cannot provide the asymmetric signature needed for code signing. Code signing requires a hash function combined with an asymmetric signing algorithm (RSA or ECDSA). Using AES-256 to encrypt a symmetric key requires the two parties to already share the AES key, which is circular — asymmetric encryption exists precisely to address the key distribution problem when no prior shared secret exists.
Why D is wrong: RSA-1024 was deprecated by NIST in 2013 and is considered cryptographically broken for most applications — it should never be selected for new deployments. The performance argument for RSA-1024 is not sound justification for using an insecure key length. MD5 is a cryptographically broken hash function with known practical collision attacks — using MD5 in code signing means an attacker could potentially forge a signed binary that produces the same MD5 digest as a legitimate one. MD5 has been prohibited in code signing and certificate contexts for over a decade. Diffie-Hellman key exchange establishes a shared secret between two parties — it is a key agreement protocol, not an encryption mechanism for transmitting a specific symmetric key to a recipient. To encrypt a specific symmetric key for transmission to a named recipient, the sender needs the recipient's public key to perform asymmetric encryption (RSA or ECIES), not a DH exchange. RSA-1024 and ECC are not equivalent in security — a 256-bit ECC key is equivalent to approximately 3072-bit RSA, not 1024-bit RSA.
